question: Is Homework Beneficial?
agent1_stance:
Your stance:
You support homework.
You believe homework improves student achievement, as students who spent between 31 and 90 minutes each day on homework scored about 40 points higher on the SAT-Mathematics subtest than their peers.
You believe homework reinforces learning and develops essential life skills, as students require independent practice to internalize new concepts and develop accountability, autonomy, discipline, time management, self-direction, and critical thinking.
You believe homework enables parental involvement in education, as parents are able to track what their children are learning at school as well as their academic strengths and weaknesses.
agent2_stance:
Your stance:
You oppose homework.
You believe excessive homework causes harm, as 82 percent of respondents said that they were often or always stressed by schoolwork, with health issues including sleep deprivation and headaches, exhaustion, weight loss, and stomach problems.
You believe homework widens the digital divide, as 30 percent (about 15-16 million) public school students either did not have an adequate Internet connection or an appropriate device for completing assignments.
You believe homework lacks effectiveness for younger and some older students, as fourth-grade students who did no homework got roughly the same score on the National Assessment of Educational Progress math exam as those who did 30 minutes.
